  • Supports logging and auditing.
  • Follows Open/Closed Principle.

Disadvantages

  • Large number of command classes.
  • Additional abstraction layer.
  • Can increase complexity for simple actions.

When to Use

  • Need Undo/Redo functionality.
  • Need task scheduling.
  • Need request logging.
  • Need asynchronous execution.
  • Need loose coupling between sender and receiver.

When Not to Use

  • Actions are extremely simple.
  • No need for queuing or undo.
  • Additional abstraction adds unnecessary complexity.

Common Interview Questions

What problem does Command Pattern solve?

It encapsulates requests as objects and decouples senders from receivers.

What is the most common real-world example?

Remote Control and Undo/Redo functionality.

Which Java interface resembles Command Pattern?

Runnable.

What is the biggest advantage of Command Pattern?

Support for queuing, logging, and undo operations.

Interview Quick Notes

  • Command is a Behavioral Design Pattern.
  • Encapsulates requests as objects.
  • Decouples sender from receiver.
  • Supports Undo/Redo operations.
  • Supports task scheduling and queuing.
  • Runnable is a common Java example.
  • Frequently used in editors, schedulers, and messaging systems.

subtopics.name: Interpreter Pattern
subtopics.content:
Interpreter Pattern

The Interpreter Pattern is a Behavioral Design Pattern used to define the grammar of a language and provide an interpreter to evaluate sentences written in that language.

It is commonly used when applications need to process, parse, and evaluate expressions.

Why Do We Need Interpreter Pattern?

Many applications need to understand and execute custom languages or expressions.

Examples include:

  • SQL Queries
  • Regular Expressions
  • Mathematical Expressions
  • Business Rules Engines
  • Search Query Languages
  • Configuration Languages

Instead of writing complex conditional logic, Interpreter Pattern models grammar rules as classes.

Real World Example

Calculator

Suppose we want to evaluate: 


10 + 20

Instead of hardcoding logic: 


if(operator.equals("+")) {
   return a + b;
}

We can represent operators as interpreter classes.

Problem Without Interpreter Pattern


String expression =
    "10 + 20";

if(expression.contains("+")) {
    // Addition Logic
}

if(expression.contains("-")) {
    // Subtraction Logic
}

if(expression.contains("*")) {
    // Multiplication Logic
}

As grammar grows, code becomes difficult to maintain.

Interpreter Pattern Solution

Represent each grammar rule as a separate class. 


Expression
      |
      +--> NumberExpression
      |
      +--> AddExpression
      |
      +--> SubtractExpression

Complex expressions are built by combining simple expressions.

Structure of Interpreter Pattern


Client
   |
   v
Abstract Expression
   |
-------------------------
|                       |
Terminal          Non-Terminal
Expression         Expression

Key Components

Component Responsibility
Abstract Expression Defines interpret() method
Terminal Expression Represents basic values
Non-Terminal Expression Represents grammar rules
Context Stores information required for interpretation
Client Builds expression tree

Implementation

Step 1: Create Expression Interface


public interface Expression {

    int interpret();
}

Step 2: Create Terminal Expression


public class NumberExpression
        implements Expression {

    private int number;

    public NumberExpression(
            int number) {

        this.number = number;
    }

    @Override
    public int interpret() {

        return number;
    }
}

Step 3: Create Non-Terminal Expression

Addition Expression


public class AddExpression
        implements Expression {

    private Expression left;

    private Expression right;

    public AddExpression(
            Expression left,
            Expression right) {

        this.left = left;
        this.right = right;
    }

    @Override
    public int interpret() {

        return left.interpret()
                + right.interpret();
    }
}

Step 4: Client Code


Expression ten =
    new NumberExpression(10);

Expression twenty =
    new NumberExpression(20);

Expression expression =
    new AddExpression(
        ten,
        twenty
    );

System.out.println(
    expression.interpret()
);

Output


30

How It Works

  1. Create terminal expressions.
  2. Create non-terminal expressions.
  3. Build an expression tree.
  4. Call interpret() on root node.
  5. Evaluation occurs recursively.

Expression Tree Example

Expression: 


10 + 20 - 5

Expression Tree: 


        (-)
       /   \
     (+)    5
    /   \
  10    20

Each node interprets itself recursively.

Class Diagram


                Expression
                     ^
                     |
        --------------------------
        |                        |
NumberExpression       AddExpression
                        |
                 Expression Tree

SQL Parser Example

Consider: 


SELECT * FROM USERS
WHERE AGE > 18

A database engine interprets SQL grammar and converts it into executable operations.

This is a real-world application of Interpreter Pattern.

Regular Expression Example

Regex engines interpret patterns such as: 


[a-z]+@[a-z]+\.com

Each grammar rule is represented internally using expression trees.

Interpreter Pattern in Spring

Interpreter Pattern is not heavily used directly in Spring applications.

However, it appears in:

  • Spring Expression Language (SpEL)
  • Spring Security Expressions
  • Query DSLs

Spring Expression Language (SpEL)


@Value("#{2 + 3}")
private int result;

Spring interprets the expression and evaluates it at runtime.

Spring Security Expressions


@PreAuthorize(
   "hasRole('ADMIN')"
)

The expression is parsed and interpreted dynamically.

Real Life Use Cases

  • SQL Parsers.
  • Regex Engines.
  • Expression Evaluators.
  • Rule Engines.
  • Compilers.
  • Configuration Parsers.
  • Spring Expression Language.

Advantages

  • Easy to extend grammar.
  • Supports complex expressions.
  • Follows Open/Closed Principle.
  • Grammar rules are reusable.
  • Easy to represent recursive structures.

Disadvantages

  • Large number of classes.
  • Complex grammar leads to complex design.
  • Performance may degrade for large expressions.
  • Difficult to maintain huge languages.

When to Use

  • Need to interpret expressions.
  • Grammar is relatively simple.
  • Language rules change frequently.
  • Expression trees are useful.

When Not to Use

  • Grammar is extremely complex.
  • Performance is critical.
  • Parser generators are more suitable.

Common Interview Questions

What problem does Interpreter Pattern solve?

It provides a way to represent and evaluate grammar rules using objects.

What are Terminal and Non-Terminal Expressions?

Terminal expressions represent basic values, while Non-Terminal expressions represent grammar rules.

What is the most common real-world example?

SQL Parsers and Regular Expression Engines.

Where is Interpreter Pattern used in Spring?

Spring Expression Language (SpEL).

Interview Quick Notes

  • Interpreter is a Behavioral Design Pattern.
  • Used for parsing and evaluating expressions.
  • Represents grammar rules as classes.
  • Uses Terminal and Non-Terminal Expressions.
  • SQL Parsers and Regex Engines are common examples.
  • SpEL is a Spring example.
  • Works well for small and medium-sized grammars.

subtopics.name: Iterator Pattern
subtopics.content:
Iterator Pattern

The Iterator Pattern is a Behavioral Design Pattern that provides a way to access elements of a collection sequentially without exposing its internal structure.

It allows clients to traverse collections such as Lists, Arrays, Sets, Trees, and Maps without knowing how the collection is implemented internally.

Why Do We Need Iterator Pattern?

Collections can have different internal implementations.

Examples:

  • ArrayList
  • LinkedList
  • HashSet
  • TreeSet
  • HashMap

Without Iterator Pattern, client code would need to understand the internal structure of every collection.

Real World Example

TV Remote

When browsing

subtopics.name: Observer Pattern
subtopics.content:
Observer Pattern

The Observer Pattern is a Behavioral Design Pattern that defines a one-to-many dependency between objects so that when one object changes state, all its dependent objects are automatically notified and updated.

It is commonly used in event-driven systems, messaging systems, UI frameworks, and distributed applications.

Why Do We Need Observer Pattern?

Many applications require multiple components to react whenever a particular event occurs.

Examples:

  • Stock Price Updates
  • Email Notifications
  • Order Status Updates
  • Social Media Followers
  • Event Publishing Systems
  • Microservice Events

Without Observer Pattern, the object generating the event must manually notify every dependent object.

Real World Example

YouTube Channel Subscription

Suppose you subscribe to a YouTube channel.

When the creator uploads a new video: 


Creator Uploads Video
         |
         v
    Subscribers
    /    |     \
User1 User2 User3

All subscribers automatically receive notifications.

The YouTube Channel is the Subject and Subscribers are Observers.

Problem Without Observer Pattern


public class StockMarket {

    private EmailService email;

    private SMSService sms;

    private MobileApp app;

    public void priceChanged() {

        email.notifyUser();

        sms.notifyUser();

        app.notifyUser();
    }
}

The StockMarket class is tightly coupled with all notification services.

Adding a new notification channel requires modifying existing code.

Observer Pattern Solution

Create a Subject that maintains a list of Observers.

Whenever state changes:

  1. Subject updates itself.
  2. Subject notifies all observers.
  3. Observers react independently.

Subject
   |
   +--> Observer 1
   |
   +--> Observer 2
   |
   +--> Observer 3

Structure of Observer Pattern


          Subject
             |
    -------------------
    |        |        |
Observer Observer Observer

Key Components

Component Responsibility
Subject Maintains observers
Observer Receives updates
Concrete Subject Stores actual state
Concrete Observer Handles notifications

Implementation

Step 1: Create Observer Interface


public interface Observer {

    void update(
        String message
    );
}

Step 2: Create Concrete Observer


public class EmailSubscriber
        implements Observer {

    @Override
    public void update(
            String message) {

        System.out.println(
            "Email Received: "
            + message
        );
    }
}

Step 3: Create Subject Interface


public interface Subject {

    void addObserver(
        Observer observer
    );

    void removeObserver(
        Observer observer
    );

    void notifyObservers();
}

Step 4: Create Concrete Subject


import java.util.ArrayList;
import java.util.List;

public class YouTubeChannel
        implements Subject {

    private List<Observer>
            observers =
            new ArrayList<>();

    private String videoTitle;

    @Override
    public void addObserver(
            Observer observer) {

        observers.add(observer);
    }

    @Override
    public void removeObserver(
            Observer observer) {

        observers.remove(observer);
    }

    @Override
    public void notifyObservers() {

        for(Observer observer
                : observers) {

            observer.update(
                videoTitle
            );
        }
    }

    public void uploadVideo(
            String title) {

        this.videoTitle = title;

        notifyObservers();
    }
}

Step 5: Client Code


YouTubeChannel channel =
        new YouTubeChannel();

Observer user1 =
        new EmailSubscriber();

Observer user2 =
        new EmailSubscriber();

channel.addObserver(user1);

channel.addObserver(user2);

channel.uploadVideo(
        "Observer Pattern"
);

Output


Email Received:
Observer Pattern

Email Received:
Observer Pattern

How It Works

  1. Observers register with Subject.
  2. Subject maintains observer list.
  3. State changes occur.
  4. Subject notifies all observers.
  5. Observers react independently.

Class Diagram


                Subject
                   |
           ----------------
           |              |
      Observer      Observer
           |
    EmailSubscriber

Push vs Pull Model

Push Model

Subject sends complete data to observers. 


subject.notify(
    "Stock Price = 100"
);

Observer immediately receives required information.

Pull Model

Subject sends minimal information. 


observer.update();

Observer pulls required data from Subject.

Stock Market Example


Stock Price Updated
         |
         v
   Stock Market
         |
 -------------------
 |        |        |
Email    SMS    Mobile App

Whenever stock prices change, all subscribers receive updates automatically.

E-Commerce Example

Product Back In Stock Notification: 


Product Available
        |
        v
Customer 1

Customer 2

Customer 3

All interested customers receive notifications.

Microservices Example

Order Created Event: 


Order Service
      |
      v
Event Bus
      |
 -------------------
 |        |        |
Email  Payment  Inventory

Each service observes and reacts independently.

Observer Pattern in Spring

Spring Events


ApplicationEventPublisher

Publishes events to registered listeners.

Publisher


publisher.publishEvent(
    new OrderCreatedEvent()
);

Observer


@EventListener
public void handleEvent(
        OrderCreatedEvent event) {

    System.out.println(
        "Event Received"
    );
}

Spring's event mechanism is a direct implementation of Observer Pattern.

Observer vs Publish Subscribe

Observer Pub/Sub
Direct relationship Uses message broker
Usually in-process Distributed systems
Tighter coupling Looser coupling

Advantages

  • Loose coupling.
  • Easy to add new observers.
  • Supports Open/Closed Principle.
  • Automatic notifications.
  • Promotes event-driven architecture.

Disadvantages

  • Unexpected update chains.
  • Debugging can be difficult.
  • Performance issues with many observers.
  • Potential memory leaks if observers are not removed.

When to Use

  • Multiple objects need updates.
  • Event-driven systems.
  • Notification systems.
  • Real-time updates.
  • Publish-subscribe style communication.

When Not to Use

  • Only one dependent object exists.
  • Updates are infrequent.
  • Simple direct method calls are sufficient.

Common Interview Questions

What problem does Observer Pattern solve?

It allows automatic notification of multiple dependent objects when state changes.

What is the difference between Observer and Pub/Sub?

Observer usually works in-process, whereas Pub/Sub uses message brokers and distributed communication.

What is the most common Spring example?

ApplicationEventPublisher and @EventListener.

Which principle does Observer Pattern support?

Open/Closed Principle.

Interview Quick Notes

  • Observer is a Behavioral Design Pattern.
  • Implements one-to-many dependency.
  • Subject notifies all observers automatically.
  • Widely used in event-driven architectures.
  • Spring Events use Observer Pattern.
  • Supports loose coupling.
  • Frequently asked in Java and Spring interviews.

subtopics.name: State Pattern
subtopics.content:
State Pattern

The State Pattern is a Behavioral Design Pattern that allows an object to change its behavior when its internal state changes.

The object appears to change its class dynamically by delegating behavior to different state objects.

Why Do We Need State Pattern?

Many applications behave differently depending on their current state.

Without State Pattern, developers often use large if-else or switch statements. 


if(state.equals("NEW")) {

    // Logic A

}
else if(state.equals("PROCESSING")) {

    // Logic B

}
else if(state.equals("COMPLETED")) {

    // Logic C

}

As states grow, the code becomes difficult to maintain and extend.

Real World Example

Traffic Signal

A traffic signal behaves differently based on its state. 


RED
 |
 v
GREEN
 |
 v
YELLOW
 |
 v
RED

Each state has different behavior and transition rules.

Problem Without State Pattern

Consider an Order Management System. 


NEW

PROCESSING

SHIPPED

DELIVERED

CANCELLED

Without State Pattern: 


if(orderStatus.equals("NEW")) {

    processOrder();

}
else if(orderStatus.equals("SHIPPED")) {

    trackOrder();

}
else if(orderStatus.equals("DELIVERED")) {

    generateInvoice();

}

Adding new states requires modifying existing code.

State Pattern Solution

Move state-specific behavior into separate state classes. 


Context
   |
   +--> New State
   |
   +--> Processing State
   |
   +--> Shipped State

The Context delegates work to the current State object.

Structure of State Pattern


Client
   |
   v
Context
   |
   v
State Interface
   |
-----------------------
|          |          |
State1   State2    State3

Key Components

Component Responsibility
Context Maintains current state
State Defines behavior contract
Concrete State Implements state-specific behavior

Implementation

Step 1: Create State Interface


public interface OrderState {

    void handleOrder(
        OrderContext context
    );
}

Step 2: Create Context


public class OrderContext {

    private OrderState state;

    public void setState(
            OrderState state) {

        this.state = state;
    }

    public void process() {

        state.handleOrder(this);
    }
}

Step 3: Create Concrete States

New State


public class NewState
        implements OrderState {

    @Override
    public void handleOrder(
            OrderContext context) {

        System.out.println(
            "Order Processing"
        );

        context.setState(
            new ProcessingState()
        );
    }
}

Processing State


public class ProcessingState
        implements OrderState {

    @Override
    public void handleOrder(
            OrderContext context) {

        System.out.println(
            "Order Shipped"
        );

        context.setState(
            new ShippedState()
        );
    }
}

Shipped State


public class ShippedState
        implements OrderState {

    @Override
    public void handleOrder(
            OrderContext context) {

        System.out.println(
            "Order Delivered"
        );
    }
}

Step 4: Client Code


OrderContext order =
        new OrderContext();

order.setState(
        new NewState()
);

order.process();

order.process();

order.process();

Output


Order Processing

Order Shipped

Order Delivered

How It Works

  1. Context maintains the current state.
  2. Client invokes operations on Context.
  3. Context delegates execution to current State.
  4. State performs behavior.
  5. State may transition Context to another State.

Class Diagram


                 Client
                    |
                    v
                Context
                    |
                    v
                 State
                    ^
                    |
     --------------------------------
     |              |              |
 NewState   ProcessingState   ShippedState

Order Management Example


NEW
 |
 v
PROCESSING
 |
 v
SHIPPED
 |
 v
DELIVERED

Each state determines what actions are allowed.

ATM Machine Example

ATM behavior changes based on card insertion. 


No Card
   |
Insert Card
   |
Has Card
   |
Enter PIN
   |
Authenticated

Different operations become available in different states.

Vending Machine Example


No Coin
   |
Insert Coin
   |
Coin Inserted
   |
Select Product
   |
Dispense Product

The machine behaves differently depending on its current state.

Workflow Engine Example


Draft
  |
  v
Review
  |
  v
Approved
  |
  v
Published

State Pattern is widely used in workflow management systems.

State Pattern in Spring Boot

Order Processing System


NEW

PAID

PACKED

SHIPPED

DELIVERED

Each state can be represented by a separate service implementation.

Payment Processing


PENDING

SUCCESS

FAILED

REFUNDED

State Pattern helps manage complex payment lifecycles.

State vs Strategy

State Strategy
Represents state of object Represents algorithm
State changes automatically Client selects strategy
Behavior depends on current state Behavior depends on chosen algorithm

State vs Chain of Responsibility

State Chain of Responsibility
Single active state Multiple handlers
Behavior changes with state Request flows through chain

Advantages

  • Eliminates large if-else blocks.
  • Encapsulates state-specific behavior.
  • Supports Open/Closed Principle.
  • Improves maintainability.
  • Makes state transitions explicit.

Disadvantages

  • Large number of state classes.
  • Increased design complexity.
  • Overkill for simple state machines.

When to Use

  • Object behavior depends on state.
  • Many state transitions exist.
  • Large if-else blocks are present.
  • Workflow-driven systems.
  • Finite State Machines (FSM).

When Not to Use

  • Only a few states exist.
  • State transitions are very simple.
  • Simple conditions are sufficient.

Common Interview Questions

What problem does State Pattern solve?

It allows objects to change behavior when their internal state changes.

What is the difference between State and Strategy?

State changes automatically during execution, whereas Strategy is chosen by the client.

What is the most common real-world example?

Order Processing Workflow and Traffic Signals.

What principle does State Pattern support?

Open/Closed Principle.

Interview Quick Notes

  • State is a Behavioral Design Pattern.
  • Encapsulates state-specific behavior.
  • Eliminates complex if-else chains.
  • Context delegates work to current State.
  • State transitions can happen dynamically.
  • Widely used in workflows and state machines.
  • Common examples: Orders, ATMs, Traffic Signals, Payment Systems.

subtopics.name: Strategy Pattern
subtopics.content:
Strategy Pattern

The Strategy Pattern is a Behavioral Design Pattern that defines a family of algorithms, encapsulates each algorithm in a separate class, and makes them interchangeable at runtime.

It allows the behavior of an object to be selected dynamically without modifying existing code.

Why Do We Need Strategy Pattern?

Applications often have multiple ways of performing the same task.

Examples:

  • Payment Methods
  • Sorting Algorithms
  • Compression Algorithms
  • Shipping Methods
  • Authentication Mechanisms

Without Strategy Pattern, developers typically use large if-else or switch statements. 


if(paymentType.equals("UPI")) {

    processUPI();

}
else if(paymentType.equals("CARD")) {

    processCard();

}
else if(paymentType.equals("NETBANKING")) {

    processNetBanking();

}

As new algorithms are added, the code becomes difficult to maintain.

Real World Example

Google Maps Navigation

Google Maps provides multiple route strategies.

  • Fastest Route
  • Shortest Route
  • Toll-Free Route
  • Bike Route

Google Maps
      |
      +--> Fastest Route
      |
      +--> Shortest Route
      |
      +--> Toll-Free Route

The user chooses a strategy and the application executes it.

Problem Without Strategy Pattern


public void pay(
        String paymentType) {

    if(paymentType.equals("UPI")) {

        processUPI();

    }
    else if(paymentType.equals("CARD")) {

        processCard();

    }
    else if(paymentType.equals("NETBANKING")) {

        processNetBanking();

    }
}

Adding a new payment method requires modifying existing code.

This violates the Open/Closed Principle.

Strategy Pattern Solution

Move each algorithm into a separate strategy class. 


Payment Strategy
      |
--------------------------
|           |            |
UPI       Card      NetBanking

The Context delegates execution to the selected strategy.

Structure of Strategy Pattern


Client
   |
   v
Context
   |
   v
Strategy
   |
-------------------------
|           |           |
S1         S2          S3

Key Components

Component Responsibility
Strategy Defines algorithm contract
Concrete Strategy Implements algorithm
Context Uses selected strategy
Client Chooses strategy

Implementation

Step 1: Create Strategy Interface


public interface PaymentStrategy {

    void pay(
        double amount
    );
}

Step 2: Create Concrete Strategies

UPI Payment


public class UpiPayment
        implements PaymentStrategy {

    @Override
    public void pay(
            double amount) {

        System.out.println(
            "Paid using UPI : "
            + amount
        );
    }
}

Card Payment


public class CardPayment
        implements PaymentStrategy {

    @Override
    public void pay(
            double amount) {

        System.out.println(
            "Paid using Card : "
            + amount
        );
    }
}

Net Banking Payment


public class NetBankingPayment
        implements PaymentStrategy {

    @Override
    public void pay(
            double amount) {

        System.out.println(
            "Paid using Net Banking : "
            + amount
        );
    }
}

Step 3: Create Context


public class PaymentService {

    private PaymentStrategy
            paymentStrategy;

    public void setStrategy(
            PaymentStrategy strategy) {

        this.paymentStrategy =
                strategy;
    }

    public void pay(
            double amount) {

        paymentStrategy.pay(
                amount);
    }
}

Step 4: Client Code


PaymentService service =
        new PaymentService();

service.setStrategy(
        new UpiPayment()
);

service.pay(1000);

Output


Paid using UPI : 1000

How It Works

  1. Client selects a strategy.
  2. Context stores the strategy.
  3. Context delegates execution to strategy.
  4. Strategy performs the algorithm.

Class Diagram


                 Client
                    |
                    v
                 Context
                    |
                    v
                 Strategy
                    ^
                    |
    ---------------------------------
    |              |               |
  UPI         CardPayment    NetBanking

Payment Gateway Example


Checkout
   |
   +--> UPI
   |
   +--> Credit Card
   |
   +--> Wallet
   |
   +--> Net Banking

Different payment algorithms can be selected at runtime.

Sorting Algorithm Example


Sort Context
      |
---------------------
|         |         |
Quick    Merge    Bubble
Sort      Sort     Sort

The client chooses the most suitable sorting strategy.

Compression Example


File Compressor
      |
---------------------
|         |         |
ZIP      RAR       GZIP

Each compression technique becomes a separate strategy.

Strategy Pattern in Spring Boot

Multiple Service Implementations


public interface NotificationService {

    void send();
}


@Service
public class EmailNotificationService
        implements NotificationService {
}


@Service
public class SMSNotificationService
        implements NotificationService {
}

Different implementations represent different strategies.

Payment Gateway Integration


PaymentStrategy
      |
-------------------------
|           |           |
Razorpay   Stripe    PayPal

Spring applications frequently use Strategy Pattern for payment processing.

Strategy vs State

Strategy State
Represents an algorithm Represents a state
Selected by client Changes automatically
Focuses on behavior selection Focuses on state transitions

Strategy vs Command

Strategy Command
Encapsulates algorithm Encapsulates request
Chooses how work is done Represents work to be done

Advantages

  • Eliminates large if-else blocks.
  • Supports Open/Closed Principle.
  • Algorithms become reusable.
  • Easy to add new strategies.
  • Runtime algorithm selection.

Disadvantages

  • Increases number of classes.
  • Client must understand available strategies.
  • Can be overkill for simple logic.

When to Use

  • Multiple algorithms exist.
  • Runtime algorithm selection is needed.
  • Large if-else blocks exist.
  • Need extensible business logic.

When Not to Use

  • Only one algorithm exists.
  • Logic is very simple.
  • Additional abstraction adds complexity.

Common Interview Questions

What problem does Strategy Pattern solve?

It allows algorithms to be encapsulated and selected dynamically at runtime.

What is the difference between Strategy and State?

Strategy focuses on algorithm selection, while State focuses on behavior changes due to state transitions.

What is the most common real-world example?

Payment Gateway Integration.

Which SOLID principle does Strategy support?

Open/Closed Principle.

Interview Quick Notes

  • Strategy is a Behavioral Design Pattern.
  • Encapsulates algorithms into separate classes.
  • Allows runtime algorithm selection.
  • Eliminates large if-else chains.
  • Widely used in payment gateways.
  • Frequently used in Spring Boot service implementations.
  • Supports Open/Closed Principle.

subtopics.name: Template Method Pattern
subtopics.content:
Template Method Pattern

The Template Method Pattern is a Behavioral Design Pattern that defines the skeleton of an algorithm in a base class while allowing subclasses to override specific steps without changing the overall algorithm structure.

It promotes code reuse by placing common workflow logic in a parent class and customizable behavior in child classes.

Why Do We Need Template Method Pattern?

Many applications follow a fixed workflow where some steps are common and others vary.

Examples:

  • Payment Processing
  • Report Generation
  • Data Parsing
  • File Processing
  • Spring Framework Lifecycle

Without Template Method Pattern, duplicate workflow logic appears across multiple classes.

Real World Example

Making Tea and Coffee

Both tea and coffee preparation follow a similar process: 


Boil Water
      |
      v
Prepare Drink
      |
      v
Pour Into Cup
      |
      v
Add Ingredients

The overall algorithm remains the same.

Only the drink preparation and ingredients differ.

Problem Without Template Method Pattern


TeaMaker

boilWater()

addTea()

pourIntoCup()

addSugar()


CoffeeMaker

boilWater()

addCoffee()

pourIntoCup()

addMilk()

Common logic such as boiling water and pouring into a cup is duplicated.

Template Method Solution

Move common workflow into a base class and allow subclasses to customize specific steps. 


Beverage
    |
---------------------
|                   |
Tea              Coffee

Structure of Template Method Pattern


Client
   |
   v
Abstract Class
   |
---------------------
|                   |
Tea            Coffee

Key Components

Component Responsibility
Abstract Class Defines template method
Template Method Defines algorithm skeleton
Concrete Class Implements variable steps

Implementation

Step 1: Create Abstract Class


public abstract class Beverage {

    public final void prepareDrink() {

        boilWater();

        brew();

        pourIntoCup();

        addIngredients();
    }

    private void boilWater() {

        System.out.println(
            "Boiling Water"
        );
    }

    private void pourIntoCup() {

        System.out.println(
            "Pouring Into Cup"
        );
    }

    protected abstract void brew();

    protected abstract void addIngredients();
}

Step 2: Create Tea Class


public class Tea
        extends Beverage {

    @Override
    protected void brew() {

        System.out.println(
            "Adding Tea Leaves"
        );
    }

    @Override
    protected void addIngredients() {

        System.out.println(
            "Adding Sugar"
        );
    }
}

Step 3: Create Coffee Class


public class Coffee
        extends Beverage {

    @Override
    protected void brew() {

        System.out.println(
            "Adding Coffee Powder"
        );
    }

    @Override
    protected void addIngredients() {

        System.out.println(
            "Adding Milk"
        );
    }
}

Step 4: Client Code


Beverage beverage =
        new Tea();

beverage.prepareDrink();

Output


Boiling Water

Adding Tea Leaves

Pouring Into Cup

Adding Sugar

How It Works

  1. Client invokes template method.
  2. Template method executes fixed workflow.
  3. Variable steps are delegated to subclasses.
  4. Subclasses provide custom implementations.

Class Diagram


                 Client
                    |
                    v
                Beverage
                    |
      -------------------------
      |                       |
     Tea                   Coffee

Hook Methods

A Hook is an optional method that subclasses may override. 


protected boolean
customerWantsSugar() {

    return true;
}

Template methods can use hooks to customize workflow behavior.

Report Generation Example


Generate Report
       |
       v
Read Data
       |
       v
Process Data
       |
       v
Generate Output

Different reports can implement their own processing logic while following the same workflow.

File Parsing Example


Read File
     |
     v
Parse Data
     |
     v
Validate Data
     |
     v
Store Data

CSV, XML, and JSON parsers may override parsing logic while keeping the same workflow.

Template Method in Spring Framework

Spring heavily uses Template Method Pattern.

JdbcTemplate


jdbcTemplate.query(
    sql,
    rowMapper
);

JdbcTemplate handles:

  • Connection Creation
  • Statement Creation
  • Exception Handling
  • Resource Cleanup

Developers provide only query-specific logic.

RestTemplate


restTemplate.getForObject(
    url,
    User.class
);

RestTemplate manages:

  • HTTP Connections
  • Serialization
  • Error Handling
  • Response Processing

AbstractController

Spring MVC internally uses Template Method concepts in controller execution flow.

Template Method vs Strategy

Template Method Strategy
Uses inheritance Uses composition
Workflow fixed in parent Algorithm selected dynamically
Compile-time customization Runtime customization

Template Method vs Command

Template Method Command
Defines workflow Encapsulates request
Inheritance based Composition based

Advantages

  • Promotes code reuse.
  • Eliminates duplicate workflow code.
  • Supports Open/Closed Principle.
  • Provides consistent algorithm structure.
  • Easy to extend behavior.

Disadvantages

  • Relies heavily on inheritance.
  • Changes to base class affect all subclasses.
  • Can become difficult to maintain with deep hierarchies.

When to Use

  • Workflow is fixed.
  • Only a few steps vary.
  • Code duplication exists across classes.
  • Need reusable process templates.

When Not to Use

  • Workflow changes frequently.
  • Runtime behavior selection is required.
  • Composition-based solutions are preferred.

Common Interview Questions

What problem does Template Method solve?

It defines a fixed algorithm structure while allowing subclasses to customize individual steps.

What is the most common Spring example?

JdbcTemplate and RestTemplate.

What is a Hook Method?

An optional method that subclasses may override to customize behavior.

What is the difference between Template Method and Strategy?

Template Method uses inheritance and fixed workflow, while Strategy uses composition and runtime algorithm selection.

Interview Quick Notes

  • Template Method is a Behavioral Design Pattern.
  • Defines algorithm skeleton in a parent class.
  • Allows subclasses to customize specific steps.
  • Promotes code reuse.
  • Uses inheritance.
  • JdbcTemplate and RestTemplate are classic Spring examples.
  • Supports Open/Closed Principle.

subtopics.name: Visitor Pattern
subtopics.content:
Visitor Pattern

The Visitor Pattern is a Behavioral Design Pattern that allows adding new operations to existing object structures without modifying those objects.

It separates algorithms from the objects on which they operate.

Why Do We Need Visitor Pattern?

Consider a system containing multiple object types. 


Circle

Rectangle

Triangle

Now suppose we need to perform different operations:

  • Calculate Area
  • Calculate Perimeter
  • Export to JSON
  • Export to XML
  • Generate Reports

Without Visitor Pattern, we would continuously modify these classes whenever a new operation is added.

This violates the Open/Closed Principle.

Real World Example

Tax Calculation System

Different item types exist:

  • Book
  • Electronics
  • Food

Different operations may be required:

  • Tax Calculation
  • Discount Calculation
  • Invoice Generation

Instead of modifying product classes, visitors perform these operations.

Problem Without Visitor Pattern


class Book {

    calculateTax();

    calculateDiscount();

    exportJson();

    exportXml();
}

As operations increase, classes become bloated.

Every new operation requires modifying all existing classes.

Visitor Pattern Solution

Move operations into separate Visitor classes. 


Elements
   |
   +--> Book
   |
   +--> Electronics
   |
   +--> Food

Visitors
   |
   +--> TaxVisitor
   |
   +--> DiscountVisitor
   |
   +--> InvoiceVisitor

New operations can be added without modifying existing element classes.

Structure of Visitor Pattern


Client
   |
   v
Visitor
   |
-------------------------
|           |           |
Book     Product    Food

Key Components

Component Responsibility
Visitor Defines operations
Concrete Visitor Implements operations
Element Accepts visitor
Concrete Element Specific object type

Implementation

Step 1: Create Visitor Interface


public interface Visitor {

    void visit(Book book);

    void visit(Electronics electronics);
}

Step 2: Create Element Interface


public interface Item {

    void accept(
        Visitor visitor
    );
}

Step 3: Create Concrete Elements

Book


public class Book
        implements Item {

    private double price;

    public Book(
            double price) {

        this.price = price;
    }

    public double getPrice() {

        return price;
    }

    @Override
    public void accept(
            Visitor visitor) {

        visitor.visit(this);
    }
}

Electronics


public class Electronics
        implements Item {

    private double price;

    public Electronics(
            double price) {

        this.price = price;
    }

    public double getPrice() {

        return price;
    }

    @Override
    public void accept(
            Visitor visitor) {

        visitor.visit(this);
    }
}

Step 4: Create Concrete Visitor


public class TaxVisitor
        implements Visitor {

    @Override
    public void visit(
            Book book) {

        System.out.println(
            "Book Tax : "
            + book.getPrice() * 0.05
        );
    }

    @Override
    public void visit(
            Electronics electronics) {

        System.out.println(
            "Electronics Tax : "
            + electronics.getPrice() * 0.18
        );
    }
}

Step 5: Client Code


Item book =
    new Book(1000);

Item laptop =
    new Electronics(50000);

Visitor taxVisitor =
    new TaxVisitor();

book.accept(taxVisitor);

laptop.accept(taxVisitor);

Output


Book Tax : 50.0

Electronics Tax : 9000.0

How It Works

  1. Client creates Visitor.
  2. Client passes Visitor to Element.
  3. Element accepts Visitor.
  4. Visitor executes type-specific logic.
  5. Operation remains outside Element class.

Double Dispatch

Visitor Pattern uses Double Dispatch.

Method selection depends on:

  • Visitor Type
  • Element Type

book.accept(visitor);

visitor.visit(book);

Java resolves both types during execution.

Class Diagram


                 Visitor
                    ^
                    |
              TaxVisitor

                    ^
                    |
                  Item
                 /    \
              Book  Electronics

Compiler Example

Compilers frequently use Visitor Pattern. 


Syntax Tree
      |
      +--> Variable Node
      |
      +--> Function Node
      |
      +--> Expression Node

Visitors perform:

  • Code Generation
  • Optimization
  • Validation
  • Static Analysis

Report Generation Example


Employee

Manager

Department

Visitors can generate:

  • PDF Reports
  • Excel Reports
  • HTML Reports

Without changing the domain classes.

Visitor Pattern in Spring

Visitor Pattern is not directly visible in Spring APIs but appears internally in:

  • Bean Processing
  • AST Processing
  • Expression Parsing
  • Annotation Processing

Spring Expression Language (SpEL)

SpEL builds expression trees and visitors process nodes during evaluation. 


#{user.age > 18}

Hibernate Query Parsing

Hibernate traverses query trees using visitor-like implementations.

Visitor vs Strategy

Visitor Strategy
Adds operations Changes algorithms
Works on object structures Works on a single context
Uses double dispatch Uses composition

Visitor vs Command

Visitor Command
Adds operations to objects Encapsulates requests
Traverses object structures Executes actions

Advantages

  • Add new operations without modifying existing classes.
  • Supports Open/Closed Principle.
  • Separates business logic from object structure.
  • Improves maintainability.
  • Useful for tree-like structures.

Disadvantages

  • Difficult to add new element types.
  • Large number of visitor methods.
  • Can increase complexity.
  • Requires understanding of double dispatch.

When to Use

  • Object structure is stable.
  • Operations change frequently.
  • Need multiple unrelated operations.
  • Tree structures exist.
  • Compiler-like systems.

When Not to Use

  • New element types are added frequently.
  • Object structure changes regularly.
  • Simple method calls are sufficient.

Common Interview Questions

What problem does Visitor Pattern solve?

It allows adding new operations without modifying existing object classes.

What is Double Dispatch?

Method selection depends on both the Visitor type and Element type.

What is the most common use case?

Compilers, AST Processing, and Report Generation.

Which principle does Visitor support?

Open/Closed Principle.

Interview Quick Notes

  • Visitor is a Behavioral Design Pattern.
  • Adds operations without modifying existing classes.
  • Uses Visitor and Element hierarchy.
  • Relies on Double Dispatch.
  • Common in Compilers and AST Processing.
  • Works best when object structure is stable.
  • Supports Open/Closed Principle.

subtopics.name: DNS Overview
subtopics.content: The Domain Name System (DNS) is a hierarchical naming system that translates human-readable domain names into IP addresses, enabling browsers to locate and communicate with web servers.

subtopics.name: DNS Resolver
subtopics.content: A DNS Resolver, also known as a Recursive Resolver, receives DNS queries from clients and performs the lookup process on their behalf. It communicates with Root Servers, TLD Servers, and Authoritative Name Servers to resolve a domain name into an IP address.

subtopics.name: Root DNS Server
subtopics.content: Root DNS Servers are the first step in DNS resolution. They do not store the IP address of a domain but direct the DNS Resolver to the appropriate Top-Level Domain (TLD) server based on the domain extension such as .com, .org, or .net.

subtopics.name: Top-Level Domain (TLD) Server
subtopics.content: TLD Servers manage information about domains within a specific top-level domain, such as .com or .org. They provide the DNS Resolver with the address of the Authoritative Name Server responsible for the requested domain.

subtopics.name: Authoritative Name Server
subtopics.content: An Authoritative Name Server stores the actual DNS records for a domain. When queried, it returns the requested information, such as the IP address associated with a domain name.

subtopics.name: DNS Resolution Process
subtopics.content: When a user enters a domain name such as telusko.com, the DNS Resolver first queries a Root DNS Server. The Root Server responds with the location of the appropriate TLD Server (for example, .com). The Resolver then queries the TLD Server, which returns the address of the Authoritative Name Server. Finally, the Resolver queries the Authoritative Name Server and receives the domain's IP address, which is returned to the user's browser.

subtopics.name: DNS Caching
subtopics.content: DNS Caching stores previously resolved domain names and their IP addresses for a specified Time To Live (TTL). This reduces lookup time and minimizes the number of requests sent to DNS servers.

subtopics.name: DNS Records
subtopics.content: DNS Records are entries stored in DNS servers that provide information about a domain. Common record types include A Records (IPv4 addresses), AAAA Records (IPv6 addresses), CNAME Records (aliases), MX Records (mail servers), and TXT Records (text-based information).

subtopics.name: What is Caching?
subtopics.content: Caching is a technique used to store frequently accessed data temporarily so that future requests can be served faster. Think of it like keeping your favorite snacks close by instead of going to the store every time.

subtopics.name: Why Caching is Important
subtopics.content: Caching improves performance and reduces load on databases or servers. By reusing stored data, applications respond faster and handle more users efficiently.

subtopics.name: Types of Caching
subtopics.content:
There are several types of caching:

  • Memory Cache: Data stored in RAM for quick access.
  • Disk Cache: Data stored on disk to persist between sessions.
  • Browser Cache: Stores web content locally to speed up page loading.
  • Application Cache: Used by software applications to store computation results.

subtopics.name: Cache Expiration and TTL
subtopics.content: Cached data is not permanent. Each item has a Time To Live (TTL) or expiration time. Once expired, the cache must fetch fresh data from the source.

subtopics.name: Cache Invalidation
subtopics.content: Cache invalidation is the process of removing outdated or incorrect data from the cache. It ensures users always get the most accurate and up-to-date information.

subtopics.name: Cache Best Practices
subtopics.content:
Some best practices include:

  • Only cache frequently accessed data.
  • Set appropriate TTL values to balance freshness and performance.
  • Use distributed caches for large applications.
  • Monitor cache hit/miss rates to optimize performance.

subtopics.name: Real-World Examples
subtopics.content: Examples of caching include web browser cache for images, CDNs caching static website content, Redis or Memcached storing session data, and CPU cache storing instructions for faster processing.

subtopics.name: Read Through Cache (RTC)
subtopics.content: Read Through Cache is a caching strategy where the cache sits between the application and the database. The application always queries the cache first. If the data is not present, the cache itself fetches it from the database, stores it, and returns it to the application.

subtopics.name: Cache Hit in Read Through Cache
subtopics.content:
In a cache hit scenario, the requested data is already present in the cache. The cache directly returns the data to the application without contacting the database, resulting in very fast response times.

  • Application → Cache → Response

subtopics.name: Cache Miss in Read Through Cache
subtopics.content:
In a cache miss scenario, the cache does not contain the requested data. The cache fetches the data from the database, stores it internally, and then returns it to the application.

  • Application → Cache → Database → Cache → Application

subtopics.name: Write Through Cache (WTC)
subtopics.content: Write Through Cache is a strategy where every write operation is first written to the cache and then immediately written to the database. This ensures strong consistency between cache and database.

subtopics.name: Write Through Cache Flow
subtopics.content:
In Write Through Cache, when data is updated, it is written simultaneously to both cache and database. This ensures the cache always stays in sync with the database but may introduce higher write latency.

  • Client → Backend → Cache → Database

subtopics.name: Write Aside Cache (Cache Aside Pattern)
subtopics.content: Write Aside Cache is a strategy where the application writes directly to the database and then updates or invalidates the cache separately. It is the most commonly used caching pattern in real-world systems.

subtopics.name: Write Aside Cache Read Flow
subtopics.content:
On a cache hit, data is returned directly from cache. On a cache miss, the application fetches data from the database, stores it in cache, and returns it to the user.

  • Cache Hit: Client → Backend → Cache → Response
  • Cache Miss: Client → Backend → Cache → DB → Cache → Response

subtopics.name: Write Aside Cache Write Flow
subtopics.content:
In Write Aside Cache, write operations update the database first. After a successful update, the cache is either invalidated or refreshed to avoid stale data.

  • Client → Backend → Database → Cache Invalidate

subtopics.name: Comparison of RTC, WTC, and WAC
subtopics.content:
• Read Through Cache: Cache handles DB fetch on miss.
  • Write Through Cache: Writes go to cache and DB simultaneously.
  • Write Aside Cache: App writes to DB first, cache updated later.

Write Aside Cache is the most widely used in modern backend systems due to its simplicity and scalability.

subtopics.name: When to Use Each Cache Strategy
subtopics.content:
• RTC: When you want cache-managed data loading.
  • WTC: When strong consistency is required.
  • WAC: When read-heavy systems need scalability and flexibility.

subtopics.name: Common  Questions
subtopics.content:
1. What is Read Through Cache?
  2. What is Write Through Cache?
  3. What is Write Aside Cache?
  4. Difference between RTC and WAC?
  5. Difference between WTC and WAC?
  6. Which caching strategy is most used in production?
  7. How does cache invalidation work in WAC?
  8. What are the risks of cache stampede?
  9. When would you avoid Write Through Cache?
  10. How do these caching strategies affect system performance?

subtopics.name: Least Recently Used (LRU)
subtopics.content: Evicts the item that has not been used for the longest time. Good for workloads where recently used items are likely to be reused.

subtopics.name: Least Frequently Used (LFU)
subtopics.content: Evicts the item that has been used the fewest times. Useful when some items are accessed more frequently than others.

subtopics.name: First In, First Out (FIFO)
subtopics.content: Evicts the oldest item in the cache. Simple to implement but may remove frequently accessed items.

subtopics.name: Random Replacement
subtopics.content: Evicts a random item. Simple and occasionally effective, especially in distributed caches where tracking LRU/LFU is expensive.

subtopics.name: Time-To-Live (TTL) / Expiration-Based
subtopics.content: Each cached item has a lifetime (TTL). Once expired, it is removed automatically. Useful for periodically changing data.

subtopics.name: Adaptive Replacement Cache (ARC)
subtopics.content: Combines LRU and LFU principles. Maintains two lists: recently used and frequently used, providing a better hit rate for mixed workloads.

subtopics.name: Segmented LRU (SLRU)
subtopics.content: Divides the cache into probationary and protected segments. Items move between segments based on usage patterns. Useful in high-performance caching systems.

subtopics.name: Most Recently Used (MRU)
subtopics.content: Evicts the most recently used item. Useful in sequential access patterns where the most recently accessed items are unlikely to be used again soon. Common in database buffer pools or sequential scans.

subtopics.name: Basics of Load Balancer
subtopics.content:
Load Balancer

A Load Balancer (LB) is a component that distributes incoming requests across multiple servers to improve availability, scalability, and reliability of an application.

Why Do We Need a Load Balancer?

Imagine an application running on a single server. As the number of users grows, the server may become overloaded and unable to handle all requests efficiently.

A load balancer sits between clients and application servers and distributes traffic among multiple servers, ensuring no single server becomes a bottleneck.

How It Works

  1. User enters a domain name in the browser.
  2. DNS resolves the domain name to the Load Balancer's IP address.
  3. The request reaches the Load Balancer.
  4. The Load Balancer selects an available server.
  5. The selected server processes the request and returns the response.

User
  |
  v
 DNS
  |
  v
Load Balancer
  |
  +----> Server 1
  |
  +----> Server 2
  |
  +----> Server 3

Main Responsibilities of a Load Balancer

1. Choosing the Server

The primary responsibility of a load balancer is deciding which server should handle the incoming request.

Common algorithms include:

  • Round Robin – Requests are distributed sequentially among servers.
  • Weighted Round Robin – Servers with higher capacity receive more requests.
  • Least Connections – Request goes to the server with the fewest active connections.
  • IP Hash – Same client IP is routed to the same server.

2. Health Checks

A load balancer continuously monitors the health of backend servers.

If a server becomes unavailable or unhealthy, the load balancer stops sending requests to that server and redirects traffic to healthy servers.

Example:


Server 1 -> Healthy ✓
Server 2 -> Healthy ✓
Server 3 -> Down ✗

New requests will only be sent to Server 1 and Server 2.

Benefits of Load Balancer

  • Improves application availability.
  • Distributes traffic evenly.
  • Supports horizontal scaling.
  • Provides fault tolerance.
  • Reduces server overload.
  • Improves response time.

Types of Load Balancers

Type Layer Description
Layer 4 Transport Layer Routes traffic using IP address and port.
Layer 7 Application Layer Routes traffic based on URL, headers, cookies, etc.

Real World Example

Suppose an e-commerce website receives 100,000 users during a sale. Instead of running the application on a single server, multiple application servers are deployed. 


Customers
    |
    v
Load Balancer
    |
+---+---+---+
|   |   |   |
S1  S2  S3  S4

The Load Balancer distributes requests among all servers, allowing the application to handle heavy traffic without downtime.

Quick Notes

  • Load Balancer distributes incoming traffic across multiple servers.
  • Improves scalability, availability, and fault tolerance.
  • Performs health checks on backend servers.
  • Common algorithms: Round Robin, Weighted Round Robin, Least Connections, IP Hash.
  • Layer 4 works on IP and Port.
  • Layer 7 works on HTTP attributes like URL, Headers, and Cookies.
  • Examples: Nginx, HAProxy, AWS ELB, Google Cloud Load Balancer.

subtopics.name: Algorithms of Load Balancer
subtopics.content:
Load Balancing Algorithms

A Load Balancer receives incoming requests and decides which backend server should handle them. The decision-making process is based on a load balancing algorithm.

Different algorithms are suitable for different use cases. Choosing the wrong algorithm can lead to uneven traffic distribution, poor response times, and resource wastage.

1. Round Robin

Round Robin is the simplest and most commonly used load balancing algorithm.

Requests are distributed sequentially among available servers. 


Request 1 -> Server 1
Request 2 -> Server 2
Request 3 -> Server 3
Request 4 -> Server 1
Request 5 -> Server 2

Advantages

  • Simple to implement.
  • Very low overhead.
  • Works well when all servers have identical capacity.
  • Traffic distribution is predictable.

Disadvantages

  • Assumes all servers have equal resources.
  • Does not consider server load.
  • Long-running requests may overload some servers.
  • Poor choice when server capacities differ.

Best Use Case

Applications where all servers have similar hardware and traffic patterns.

2. Geo-Based Algorithm

Geo-based load balancing routes users to the nearest server based on geographical location. 


India User  -> Mumbai Server
US User     -> Virginia Server
Europe User -> Frankfurt Server

Location can be determined using IP geolocation.

Advantages

  • Reduces network latency.
  • Improves user experience.
  • Reduces cross-region traffic costs.
  • Supports disaster recovery across regions.

Disadvantages

  • Requires geographically distributed infrastructure.
  • IP geolocation may be inaccurate.
  • Nearby server may still be overloaded.
  • More complex to configure.

Best Use Case

Global applications such as Netflix, Amazon, Google, and Cloudflare.

3. Least Connections Algorithm

The load balancer sends the next request to the server having the fewest active connections. 


Server 1 -> 50 active connections
Server 2 -> 20 active connections
Server 3 -> 10 active connections

Next Request -> Server 3

Advantages

  • Considers current server load.
  • Works well for long-lived connections.
  • Provides better balancing than Round Robin.
  • Reduces chances of server overload.

Disadvantages

  • Requires continuous monitoring of active connections.
  • Connection count may not represent actual resource usage.
  • Slightly higher overhead.

Best Use Case

WebSocket applications, chat applications, and streaming systems.

4. Least Response Time Algorithm

Requests are routed to the server with the fastest response time.

The load balancer continuously measures response times and chooses the fastest server. 


Server 1 -> 120 ms
Server 2 -> 80 ms
Server 3 -> 50 ms

Next Request -> Server 3

Advantages

  • Provides optimal user experience.
  • Automatically adapts to changing server performance.
  • Helps avoid slow servers.
  • Improves overall response time.

Disadvantages

  • Requires continuous monitoring.
  • Higher computational overhead.
  • Response times may fluctuate rapidly.
  • More complex than Round Robin.

Best Use Case

High-performance systems where latency is critical.

5. IP Hash Algorithm

The client's IP address is hashed to determine the destination server. 


hash(Client IP) % Number of Servers

A specific user is consistently routed to the same server. 


192.168.1.10 -> Server 2
192.168.1.10 -> Server 2
192.168.1.10 -> Server 2

Advantages

  • Provides session persistence.
  • Simple implementation.
  • Useful when session data is stored locally.
  • Reduces need for distributed session storage.

Disadvantages

  • Uneven traffic distribution may occur.
  • Adding/removing servers changes hash mappings.
  • Some servers may become overloaded.
  • Not ideal for dynamic environments.

Best Use Case

Legacy applications that depend on sticky sessions.

6. Weighted Round Robin

Weighted Round Robin is an enhancement of Round Robin where servers receive traffic according to their assigned weights. 


Server 1 Weight = 5
Server 2 Weight = 3
Server 3 Weight = 2

Traffic Distribution:

S1 S1 S1 S1 S1
S2 S2 S2
S3 S3

A more powerful server receives more requests.

Advantages

  • Handles heterogeneous infrastructure.
  • Better utilization of powerful servers.
  • Simple implementation.
  • More balanced than basic Round Robin.

Disadvantages

  • Weights must be configured manually.
  • Cannot react to real-time server load.
  • Incorrect weights can create imbalance.

Best Use Case

Clusters where servers have different CPU, RAM, or hardware capacities.

Hybrid Approach

Modern load balancers rarely rely on a single algorithm. Instead, they combine multiple algorithms to make smarter routing decisions.

Example 1: Geo + Least Connections


Step 1: Route user to nearest region.
Step 2: Select server with least connections.

Used by global cloud providers.

Example 2: Weighted Round Robin + Health Checks


Server 1 Weight = 5
Server 2 Weight = 3
Server 3 Weight = 2

If Server 1 becomes unhealthy,
all traffic is redistributed to healthy servers.

Example 3: IP Hash + Least Response Time

Maintain session affinity while preferring servers with better performance.

Advantages of Hybrid Approach

  • More intelligent routing.
  • Better scalability.
  • Improved fault tolerance.
  • Optimized performance and latency.
  • Can adapt to changing traffic conditions.

Disadvantages of Hybrid Approach

  • More complex configuration.
  • Higher monitoring overhead.
  • Requires advanced load balancers.

Comparison Table

Algorithm Considers Load Session Persistence Complexity
Round Robin No No Low
Weighted Round Robin Partially No Low
Least Connections Yes No Medium
Least Response Time Yes No High
IP Hash No Yes Medium
Geo Based Region Level Optional High

Summary

  • Round Robin is the simplest and most commonly used algorithm.
  • Weighted Round Robin is suitable when servers have different capacities.
  • Least Connections is preferred for long-lived connections.
  • Least Response Time focuses on latency optimization.
  • IP Hash provides sticky sessions.
  • Geo-Based Routing improves global user experience.
  • Modern cloud systems commonly use hybrid load balancing strategies.

subtopics.name: Health Checks in Load Balancer
subtopics.content:
Health Checks in Load Balancers

A Load Balancer continuously monitors the health of backend servers to ensure traffic is only routed to healthy instances.

Without health checks, requests may be sent to failed or overloaded servers, resulting in application downtime and poor user experience.

Why Are Health Checks Needed?

Consider a system with three application servers. 


Load Balancer
     |
 +---+---+---+
 |   |   |   |
S1  S2  S3

If Server S2 crashes but the Load Balancer is unaware of the failure, it will continue sending requests to S2, causing request failures.

Health checks allow the Load Balancer to automatically detect unhealthy servers and remove them from the traffic pool.

How Health Checks Work

  1. Load Balancer periodically sends health check requests.
  2. Server responds with success or failure.
  3. Load Balancer tracks consecutive successes and failures.
  4. If failure threshold is reached, the server is marked unhealthy.
  5. If recovery threshold is reached, the server is marked healthy again.

Health Check Request
        |
        v
     Server
        |
        +--> Success -> Healthy
        |
        +--> Failure -> Unhealthy

Health Check Parameters

1. Interval

Interval defines how often the Load Balancer performs health checks.

Example: 


Interval = 5 seconds

This means every 5 seconds the Load Balancer sends a health check request to each backend server.

Advantages of Small Interval

  • Detects failures quickly.
  • Faster traffic rerouting.
  • Improves availability.

Disadvantages of Small Interval

  • Increased network traffic.
  • Higher monitoring overhead.
  • Additional load on backend servers.

Example


Interval = 5 seconds

00:00 -> Check
00:05 -> Check
00:10 -> Check
00:15 -> Check

2. Timeout

Timeout defines how long the Load Balancer waits for a response from the server.

If the server does not respond within the timeout period, the health check is considered failed. 


Timeout = 2 seconds

Request Sent
      |
      +--> Response within 2 sec -> Success
      |
      +--> No Response after 2 sec -> Failure

Example

Response Time Result
500 ms Pass
1.5 sec Pass
3 sec Fail

Choosing Timeout

  • Too small → healthy servers may be marked unhealthy.
  • Too large → failure detection becomes slow.
  • Usually smaller than the interval.

3. Threshold

Threshold determines how many consecutive successes or failures are required before changing the server's health status.

Unhealthy Threshold

Number of consecutive failed health checks required before marking a server unhealthy. 


Unhealthy Threshold = 3

Check 1 -> Fail
Check 2 -> Fail
Check 3 -> Fail

Result:
Server marked UNHEALTHY

This prevents temporary network glitches from immediately removing a server.

Healthy Threshold

Number of consecutive successful health checks required before bringing a server back into service. 


Healthy Threshold = 2

Check 1 -> Success
Check 2 -> Success

Result:
Server marked HEALTHY

This prevents unstable servers from repeatedly entering and leaving the traffic pool.

Real World Example

Assume the following configuration: 


Interval = 5 sec
Timeout = 2 sec
Unhealthy Threshold = 3
Healthy Threshold = 2

Server crashes at 10:00:00. 


10:00:05 -> Fail (1)
10:00:10 -> Fail (2)
10:00:15 -> Fail (3)

Server marked UNHEALTHY

Server recovers later. 


10:01:00 -> Success (1)
10:01:05 -> Success (2)

Server marked HEALTHY

Types of Health Checks

TCP Health Check

Checks whether a TCP connection can be established. 


Can connect to port 8080?
YES -> Healthy
NO  -> Unhealthy

HTTP Health Check

Calls a dedicated endpoint such as: 


GET /health
GET /actuator/health

Server returns HTTP 200 if healthy.

HTTPS Health Check

Same as HTTP health checks but over SSL/TLS.

Custom Health Check

Application verifies database, cache, message queues, and other dependencies before reporting healthy status.

Spring Boot Example

Spring Boot Actuator provides a built-in health endpoint. 


implementation 'org.springframework.boot:spring-boot-starter-actuator'

Health endpoint: 


GET /actuator/health

Typical response: 


{
  "status": "UP"
}

Quick Notes

  • Health checks ensure traffic is sent only to healthy servers.
  • Interval defines how often health checks run.
  • Timeout defines how long the Load Balancer waits for a response.
  • Unhealthy Threshold defines failures required to mark a server down.
  • Healthy Threshold defines successes required to mark a server healthy.
  • TCP checks verify connectivity.
  • HTTP checks verify application availability.
  • Spring Boot commonly exposes /actuator/health for Load Balancer monitoring.

subtopics.name: Basics of database replication
subtopics.content:
Database Replication

Database Replication is the process of maintaining multiple copies of the same data across different database servers.

Whenever data is written to the primary database, the changes are propagated to one or more replica databases to keep them synchronized.

Why Do We Need Database Replication?

As applications grow, a single database server becomes a bottleneck for availability, performance, and scalability.

Replication helps solve these problems by creating additional copies of data.

1. Eliminate Single Point of Failure (SPOF)

If all data exists on only one database server and that server crashes, the entire application may become unavailable. 


Application
     |
     v
 Database

Database Down = Application Down

With replication, another database copy can take over. 


Application
     |
     v
 Primary DB
     |
     v
 Replica DB

Primary Down -> Replica Takes Over

2. Improve Availability

Since multiple copies of data exist, users can continue accessing the application even if one database server becomes unavailable.

This significantly improves system uptime.

3. Improve Performance

Read requests can be distributed across multiple replica databases.

Instead of a single database handling all traffic, the workload is shared. 


            Read Requests
                 |
        +--------+--------+
        |        |        |
      Replica1 Replica2 Replica3

            Writes
              |
           Primary

This reduces load on the primary database.

4. Keep Data Close to Users

Organizations often maintain replicas in multiple geographic regions. 


US Users    -> US Replica
India Users -> India Replica
Europe Users -> EU Replica

Users access a nearby replica, reducing network latency and improving response time.

5. Increase Read Throughput

A single database can handle only a limited number of read requests.

For example: 


Single Database

Read Capacity = 10,000 requests/sec

Adding another replica increases total read capacity. 


Primary DB  = 10,000 reads/sec
Replica DB  = 10,000 reads/sec

Total Capacity = 20,000 reads/sec

This technique is heavily used in large-scale systems where reads greatly outnumber writes.

How Replication Works

The Primary Database accepts write operations.

Changes are then copied to replica databases. 


          Write
            |
            v
       Primary DB
        /   |   \
       /    |    \
      v     v     v
 Replica Replica Replica

All replicas contain the same data as the primary database.

Primary-Replica Architecture

This is the most common replication model.

Primary Database

  • Handles INSERT operations.
  • Handles UPDATE operations.
  • Handles DELETE operations.
  • Acts as source of truth.

Replica Database

  • Handles read operations.
  • Receives updates from primary.
  • Provides high availability.
  • Improves read scalability.

                Write
                  |
                  v
              Primary
                  |
        +---------+---------+
        |                   |
        v                   v
    Replica 1          Replica 2
        |                   |
      Read                Read

Real World Example

Consider an e-commerce application.

Users continuously browse products while relatively fewer users place orders. 


Read Requests  = 100,000/sec
Write Requests = 5,000/sec

If all requests hit a single database:

  • Database becomes overloaded.
  • Response times increase.
  • Application performance suffers.

Instead: 


                 Load Balancer
                        |
           +------------+------------+
           |            |            |
           v            v            v
       Replica1     Replica2     Replica3

                 ^
                 |
              Primary
              (Writes)

Reads are distributed among replicas while writes continue going to the primary database.

Replication vs Partitioning

Feature Replication Partitioning (Sharding)
Purpose Copy Data Split Data
Data Same Data Everywhere Different Data Per Shard
Improves Availability & Read Scaling Storage & Write Scaling
Example User table copied to all replicas Users split across shards

Replication + Partitioning

Large-scale systems usually combine both techniques.

Each shard maintains its own replicas. 


           Shard 1
         /    |    \
        R1   R2   R3

           Shard 2
         /    |    \
        R1   R2   R3

           Shard 3
         /    |    \
        R1   R2   R3

This provides:

  • Horizontal scaling.
  • High availability.
  • Read scalability.
  • Fault tolerance.

Advantages of Replication

  • High availability.
  • Eliminates single point of failure.
  • Improves read performance.
  • Supports disaster recovery.
  • Reduces latency by serving nearby users.
  • Improves fault tolerance.

Disadvantages of Replication

  • Additional infrastructure cost.
  • Replication lag may occur.
  • More complex architecture.
  • Data consistency becomes challenging.
  • Monitoring and maintenance overhead increases.

Quick Notes

  • Replication means maintaining multiple copies of the same data.
  • Primary database handles writes.
  • Replicas typically handle reads.
  • Main goals are availability, fault tolerance, and read scalability.
  • Replication improves read throughput but does not significantly improve write throughput.
  • Replication and partitioning solve different scaling problems.
  • Most modern distributed systems use both replication and partitioning together.

subtopics.name: Replication algorithms
subtopics.content:
Database Replication Strategies

Replication determines how data changes are propagated from one database node to other replica nodes.

Different replication strategies make different trade-offs between consistency, availability, latency, and fault tolerance.

1. Synchronous Replication

In synchronous replication, the primary database waits for replicas to acknowledge the write before confirming success to the client. 


Client
   |
 Write
   |
   v
Primary
   |
   +----> Replica 1 ✓
   |
   +----> Replica 2 ✓
   |
Success Returned

How It Works

  1. Client sends write request.
  2. Primary writes data.
  3. Replicas receive and store data.
  4. Replicas acknowledge success.
  5. Primary returns success to client.

Advantages

  • Strong consistency.
  • No data loss during failover.
  • All replicas remain synchronized.

Disadvantages

  • Higher write latency.
  • Slow replicas impact performance.
  • Lower availability.

Best Use Cases

  • Banking systems.
  • Financial transactions.
  • Critical business data.

2. Asynchronous Replication

In asynchronous replication, the primary acknowledges the client immediately after writing locally and replicates changes later. 


Client
   |
 Write
   |
   v
Primary
   |
Success Returned

Later...

Primary ---> Replica 1
Primary ---> Replica 2

How It Works

  1. Primary receives write.
  2. Data is written locally.
  3. Success returned immediately.
  4. Replication happens in background.

Advantages

  • Fast write performance.
  • Low latency.
  • High availability.
  • Scales well geographically.

Disadvantages

  • Possible data loss.
  • Replication lag.
  • Replicas may contain stale data.

Best Use Cases

  • Social media applications.
  • E-commerce systems.
  • Content platforms.

3. Semi-Synchronous Replication

Semi-synchronous replication is a hybrid approach.

The primary waits for acknowledgement from at least one replica before returning success. 


Client
   |
 Write
   |
Primary
   |
   +--> Replica 1 ✓
   |
Success Returned

Remaining replicas updated later.

Advantages

  • Better durability than asynchronous replication.
  • Lower latency than synchronous replication.
  • Balanced trade-off.

Disadvantages

  • More complex implementation.
  • Still possible to lose some data.

Best Use Cases

  • Enterprise applications.
  • Large SaaS platforms.

4. Leader-Follower Replication

Also known as Primary-Replica Replication.

Only one node accepts writes while replicas handle reads. 


            Writes
               |
               v
            Leader
          /    |    \
         /     |     \
        v      v      v
      F1      F2     F3

        Reads

Advantages

  • Simple architecture.
  • Easy conflict management.
  • Excellent read scaling.

Disadvantages

  • Leader becomes bottleneck.
  • Leader failure affects writes.

Examples

  • MySQL Replication.
  • PostgreSQL Streaming Replication.
  • Amazon RDS Read Replicas.

5. Multi-Master Replication

Multiple nodes can accept write operations simultaneously. 


      Master 1
         |
         |
      Master 2
         |
         |
      Master 3

All nodes can read and write.

Advantages

  • No single write bottleneck.
  • High availability.
  • Supports regional writes.

Disadvantages

  • Conflict resolution required.
  • Complex architecture.
  • Difficult consistency management.

Example Conflict


Master 1:
User Name = John

Master 2:
User Name = Johnny

Which value wins?

Examples

  • CouchDB.
  • Cassandra (multi-leader style).

6. Quorum-Based Replication

A write succeeds only when a minimum number of replicas acknowledge it.

Similarly, reads query multiple replicas and select the most recent version. 


N = 5 replicas

Write Quorum (W) = 3
Read Quorum (R) = 3

W + R > N

This ensures strong consistency.

Advantages

  • Strong consistency.
  • Fault tolerance.
  • No single point of failure.

Disadvantages

  • More network communication.
  • Complex implementation.
  • Higher latency.

Examples

  • Cassandra.
  • DynamoDB.
  • Riak.

7. Chain Replication

Replicas are arranged in a chain. 


Write

Head
 |
 v
Node 1
 |
 v
Node 2
 |
 v
Node 3
 |
 v
Tail

Read From Tail

Writes flow through every replica in sequence.

Advantages

  • Strong consistency.
  • High read throughput.
  • Predictable behavior.

Disadvantages

  • Higher write latency.
  • Failure handling is complex.

Examples

  • Microsoft Azure Storage (early versions).
  • Research distributed systems.

Comparison Table

Strategy Consistency Write Speed Complexity
Synchronous Very High Slow Medium
Asynchronous Low-Medium Fast Low
Semi-Synchronous High Medium Medium
Leader-Follower Depends Medium Low
Multi-Master Medium Fast High
Quorum High Medium High
Chain High Slow High

Quick Notes

  • Synchronous replication prioritizes consistency.
  • Asynchronous replication prioritizes performance.
  • Semi-synchronous provides a balance between the two.
  • Leader-Follower is the most commonly used replication architecture.
  • Multi-Master allows writes on multiple nodes but introduces conflicts.
  • Quorum replication is common in distributed databases.
  • Chain replication provides strong consistency with predictable ordering.

subtopics.name: Partition
subtopics.content:
Database Partitioning (Sharding)

Database Partitioning, also known as Sharding, is the process of splitting large datasets across multiple database servers.

Unlike replication, where the same data is copied to multiple servers, partitioning distributes different portions of data across multiple databases.

Why Do We Need Partitioning?

As applications grow, a single database eventually becomes a bottleneck.

Problems with a single database include:

  • Storage limitations.
  • High read and write traffic.
  • Slow query performance.
  • Expensive vertical scaling.

Partitioning solves these problems by distributing data across multiple servers.

Single Database Problem


               Application
                    |
                    v
               Database

Users: 100 Million
Orders: 500 Million
Products: 10 Million

Single Database becomes overloaded.

At some point adding more CPU and RAM is not enough.

What is Sharding?

Sharding divides data into smaller chunks called Shards.

Each shard stores only a subset of the total data. 


              Users Table

      +-----------------------+
      |     All Users         |
      +-----------------------+

                Split

     +---------+---------+---------+
     | Shard 1 | Shard 2 | Shard 3 |
     +---------+---------+---------+

Each shard is an independent database.

Benefits of Partitioning

1. Horizontal Scaling

Instead of upgrading a single server, new database servers can be added. 


DB1 + DB2 + DB3 + DB4

This allows the system to scale almost indefinitely.

2. Increased Write Throughput

Writes are distributed across multiple shards. 


Shard 1 -> 5,000 writes/sec
Shard 2 -> 5,000 writes/sec
Shard 3 -> 5,000 writes/sec

Total = 15,000 writes/sec

This is one of the biggest advantages over replication.

3. Reduced Query Load

Each database handles only a portion of the total data.

Queries become faster because the dataset is smaller.

4. Better Resource Utilization

Storage, CPU, and memory requirements are distributed across multiple servers.

Partitioning Strategies

1. Horizontal Partitioning

Rows are divided among multiple databases. 


Users Table

ID  Name
1   John
2   Mike
3   Sarah
4   Alex

Split Into:

Shard 1
---------
1 John
2 Mike

Shard 2
---------
3 Sarah
4 Alex

Advantages

  • Most common sharding approach.
  • Supports huge datasets.
  • Excellent scalability.

Disadvantages

  • Cross-shard queries become complex.
  • Requires shard routing logic.

2. Vertical Partitioning

Columns are divided among databases. 


User Table

ID | Name | Address | Photo

Split Into:

User DB
---------
ID | Name

Profile DB
---------
ID | Address | Photo

Advantages

  • Frequently accessed data remains lightweight.
  • Reduces unnecessary I/O.

Disadvantages

  • Requires joins across databases.
  • Increases application complexity.

Partitioning Algorithms

1. Range-Based Partitioning

Data is divided based on a range of values. 


User ID 1 - 1,000,000   -> Shard 1
User ID 1,000,001 - 2,000,000 -> Shard 2
User ID 2,000,001 - 3,000,000 -> Shard 3
